content: Hi, I'm currently checking how to setup a Linux kernel development flow for our company and RfL has been a great inspiration. I was wondering what the experience is with the rust-next branch, that seems to be regularly rebased. Is there a single person doing that? Does this cause issues in practice? It would be great if someone can share some experiences. Thanks!
Basically, rust-next contains the patches that Miguel will upstream in the next merge window. They are only added there once they are ready for being sent to Linus. Miguel maintains it.
You can read this for a rough idea about Linux kernel development process.

Thanks for the pointer, @Boqun Feng. I'm aware of the upstream development process. I was mostly wondering what the experiences are with managing a downstream repo where development happens. It's really nice that RfL doesn't use an email-based flow. :+1:
what do you mean? We use the same process as other kernel subsystems
and rust-next is not a downstream repo branch, it’s part of Linux upstream

I meant a repo where feature development happens and that submits patches upstream to Linus' tree. I'm mostly interested in the behind-the-scenes process of managing the rust-next branch. But as @Alice Ryhl pointed out, Miguel is working that magic by hand it seems.
We do, in fact, use an email-based workflow. Patches are sent to rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org. Once they are ready for being upstreamed, Miguel adds them to rust-next.

Our tree works the same way as other subsystems in the kernel -- I am not sure what you mean by rebasing rust-next. That branch is not rebased, and it uses a patch-based workflow.

In case you haven't seen it yet, please also see https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing and https://rust-for-linux.com/branches.
Ah, sorry for the confusion on my part! What's up with the GitHub PRs then?
The links I mentioned answer most of that question -- I say most because sometimes the PRs have also been used for early reviews/drafts.
Yeah, these days PRs are used only for the occasional pre-LKML review.
Also kudos to having great documentation that I should have read more carefully! Thank you for your patience. :pray:
